Class 4 · Module 1

The Small Stuff That Runs Everything: Vitamins and Minerals

Needed in milligrams, indispensable in everything, the tools, spark plugs, and signaling wires of metabolism.

31 min read 14 cited sources

In 1740, Commodore George Anson left England with roughly 1,900 men aboard a squadron bound to harass Spanish shipping in the Pacific. He returned almost four years later with a captured treasure galleon, and with fewer than 700 men. Spanish cannon had killed only a handful. The rest died of scurvy: gums rotting, old wounds reopening, men too weak to haul a line. The cure, it would turn out, was already growing on trees in every port they passed. A few oranges a day, a few dozen milligrams of a single molecule, would have saved most of them.

That is the central scandal of micronutrition. Anson's sailors were not starving. They ate thousands of calories of salt beef and ship's biscuit daily. Their problem was not fuel. It was the absence of something so small it could be measured on the tip of a knife, and without it, a body powered by abundant energy simply fell apart.

Bricks, Fuel, and Everything Else

In Chapters 2 and 3 we treated the body as a metabolic engine: carbohydrates, fats, and proteins as the fuel and the building material, oxidized through glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and the electron transport chain to spin out ATP. Those macronutrients are consumed in grams, tens to hundreds of grams a day. They are the bricks and the diesel.

But an engine is more than fuel. It needs spark plugs, lubricant, wiring, sensors. Micronutrients, vitamins and minerals, fill those roles. We need them in milligrams or even micrograms, and we cannot, with a few exceptions, manufacture them ourselves. They do almost no caloric work; you could eat a kilogram of vitamins and gain essentially no energy. Yet the energy reactions you already studied grind to a halt without them.

This chapter builds three ideas in sequence. First, the basic taxonomy: how vitamins differ from minerals, and why the fat-soluble versus water-soluble split matters for everything from storage to toxicity. Second, the unifying mechanism, micronutrients as cofactors, the molecular helpers that let enzymes do their jobs. Third, the most vivid evidence we have for why any of this matters: the flagship deficiency diseases, nature's own subtraction experiments, each one tying a single nutrient to a single, visible failure.

The Taxonomy: Vitamins Versus Minerals

The distinction is simpler than its reputation. Minerals are chemical elements, iron, zinc, calcium, iodine, magnesium, potassium. They come ultimately from rock and soil, enter the food chain through plants, and cannot be created or destroyed by cooking or digestion. An iron atom in your spinach is the same iron atom that may end up in a hemoglobin molecule.

Vitamins are organic molecules, they contain carbon, are built by living things (plants, fungi, bacteria, occasionally our own cells from precursors), and can be degraded by heat, light, or oxygen. The word itself is a historical accident: Casimir Funk coined "vitamine" in 1912 from vita (life) plus amine, believing these factors were all amines. Most aren't, so the final "e" was quietly dropped. The category is defined less by chemistry than by a job description: an organic compound the body requires in tiny amounts but cannot synthesize in sufficient quantity, so it must come from the diet (Ofoedu et al., 2021).

Fat-Soluble Versus Water-Soluble: The Most Useful Split

Of the thirteen recognized vitamins, four are fat-soluble, A, D, E, and K, and the rest are water-soluble, the eight B vitamins and vitamin C. This is not chemical trivia. As Ofoedu and colleagues (2021) emphasize, solubility governs four practical things at once: how a vitamin is absorbed, how it is stored, how toxic it can become, and how often you must eat it.

Fat-soluble vitamins ride along with dietary fat. They require bile and are packaged into the same lipid transport system that handles your dinner's olive oil. Because the body can stash them in the liver and adipose tissue, you can go for weeks or months without them, and you can overdose on them, because excess is stored rather than excreted. Vitamin A toxicity from over-supplementation is a real clinical entity.

Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in the body's aqueous compartments. They are absorbed more directly, stored poorly, and the excess is flushed out in urine. This makes acute toxicity rare but means the supply line must be continuously replenished, miss vitamin C for weeks and reserves run dry, which is precisely why scurvy struck sailors on long voyages and not landsmen who ate the occasional fresh vegetable.

Spend a moment on the four fat-soluble vitamins, because grouping them by chemistry tells you a lot about how they behave. Vitamin A (retinol and its precursor carotenoids) is needed for vision, for the integrity of skin and mucous membranes, and for immune function. Vitamin D regulates calcium absorption and bone mineralization and acts throughout the body as a hormone. Vitamin E is the body's main fat-phase antioxidant, protecting the lipids in cell membranes from oxidative damage. Vitamin K is the cofactor that lets the liver finish building several clotting factors, which is why a deficiency shows up as a tendency to bleed. All four share the same logistics: they need dietary fat and bile to be absorbed, they are carried in the body's lipid transport system, and they are banked in the liver and fatty tissue. That storage is a feature and a hazard at once. It buffers you against a few lean days, but it also means a steady oversupply, almost always from high-dose supplements rather than food, can build to a level the body cannot clear. Excess vitamin A can damage the liver and, in pregnancy, harm the developing fetus; excess vitamin D can push blood calcium dangerously high.

The water-soluble group runs on the opposite logic. The eight B vitamins and vitamin C dissolve straight into the watery compartments of the body, get absorbed without needing fat, and are held in only small reserves. Take in more than you need on a given day and most of the surplus leaves in the urine within hours. The upside is a wide margin of safety: it is genuinely hard to poison yourself with vitamin C from food, and even large oral doses are mostly excreted. The cost is that there is no real buffer. Cut off the supply and the tank runs dry on the timescale of weeks, which is the whole reason a sailor who stopped eating fresh food developed scurvy on a months-long voyage while someone eating an occasional vegetable never did. Vitamin B12 is the notable exception inside this group: the liver can store a multi-year reserve, so a B12 problem can take years to surface, a detail we will return to.

Here is the practical translation. Solubility is not a textbook label to memorize; it tells you how a nutrient is likely to fail you and how it is likely to harm you. The water-soluble vitamins are the ones to put on a fairly regular schedule, because you are constantly running them down. The fat-soluble vitamins are the ones where megadosing from pills, not the amounts in any normal plate of food, carries the real toxicity risk, because the body keeps what you do not use.

Think About It

A friend takes a single enormous "mega-dose" of vitamin C and a single mega-dose of vitamin D on the same morning. Which one is the body more likely to simply excrete, and which one is more likely to accumulate toward a toxic level over time? What property explains the difference?

The Big Idea: Micronutrients as Cofactors

Here is the concept that ties the whole chapter together and connects it back to the metabolism you already know. Most enzymes, the protein machines that catalyze the body's chemical reactions, cannot work as bare protein alone. They need a non-protein helper bolted in. That helper is a cofactor. When the cofactor is an organic molecule, we call it a coenzyme; when it is a metal ion, it is simply a metal cofactor. And the overwhelming majority of cofactors are derived directly from dietary vitamins and minerals.

Think of the enzyme as a power tool and the cofactor as the bit or blade. The tool's housing is useless without the right bit snapped into place. Remove a milligram-scale cofactor and a reaction processing thousands of calories of fuel simply stops.

The examples land right inside the pathways from Chapters 2 and 3. Niacin (B3) becomes NAD⁺/NADH, the electron-carrying coenzyme that shuttles reducing power through glycolysis and the citric acid cycle. Riboflavin (B2) becomes FAD/FADH₂, the other great electron carrier. Thiamine (B1) is the cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase, the gateway enzyme converting pyruvate into the acetyl-CoA that feeds the citric acid cycle. Pantothenic acid (B5) is the backbone of coenzyme A itself. Magnesium is required to stabilize and activate ATP, virtually every reaction that "spends" ATP actually spends a magnesium-ATP complex. And iron sits at the heart of the cytochromes of the electron transport chain, ferrying electrons toward oxygen (Huskisson et al., 2007; Tardy et al., 2020).

This is why Huskisson and colleagues (2007) frame the entire vitamin–mineral story as inseparable from energy metabolism, and why a person eating plenty of calories but short on B vitamins, iron, or magnesium so often reports the vague but real complaint of fatigue. The fuel is present; the machinery to burn it is gummed up. Tardy and colleagues (2020) review exactly this link, documenting how even subclinical shortfalls, too mild to produce a named disease, degrade energy production, cognition, and mood.

Nutrients Rarely Act Alone

One refinement keeps this from becoming a simple list to memorize. Cofactors do not operate as isolated heroes, each running one reaction. Scott-Boyer and colleagues (2016) used network analysis of cofactor–protein interactions to map how vitamins and minerals interact with hundreds of proteins across overlapping pathways. Their finding: micronutrients form a densely interconnected web. A single deficiency rarely disables one reaction cleanly; it ripples through clusters of linked processes that share that cofactor or depend on its products. Magnesium and a B vitamin and a metal ion may all be required at adjacent steps of the same pathway, drawn from entirely different foods. This is the molecular argument for dietary variety, and a preview of why "just take this one supplement" is so often the wrong mental model.

Interactive

A Closer Look: Five Nutrients Worth Studying

The cofactor idea explains why micronutrients matter in general. To make it usable, it helps to know a handful of specific nutrients well: what each one does, who tends to run low, and what a shortfall actually looks like. Five are worth studying because they are common points of failure in otherwise well-fed people: iron, vitamin D, vitamin B12, calcium, and magnesium. The point is not to turn you into a diagnostician. It is to give you a working mental model of where ordinary diets tend to leave gaps.

Iron: Oxygen Delivery and the Heme Advantage

Iron's headline job is oxygen transport, sitting at the center of hemoglobin in red blood cells, and we will return to iron-deficiency anemia as a flagship disease later in this chapter. What is worth adding here is who runs low and why the source matters. Menstruating women lose iron monthly and need substantially more than men. Pregnancy raises demand sharply. Endurance athletes lose small amounts through sweat, gut, and the mechanical breakdown of red cells, and people who eat little or no meat rely entirely on the less-absorbable form of the mineral.

That last point is the practical hinge. Dietary iron comes in two forms. Heme iron, from meat, poultry, and fish, is absorbed efficiently and is relatively unbothered by the rest of the meal. Non-heme iron, from beans, lentils, tofu, spinach, and fortified grains, is absorbed far less readily, and its uptake swings with what sits beside it on the plate. Vitamin C eaten in the same meal sharply increases non-heme absorption; the tannins in tea and coffee and the phytates in some whole grains and legumes reduce it. So a plant-based eater is not simply getting less iron, they are getting iron in the form most sensitive to meal composition. Pairing the lentils with peppers or citrus, and keeping the strong tea for between meals rather than during them, is doing real biochemical work, not just adding flavor.

Vitamin D: The Sunlight Hormone With Few Food Sources

Vitamin D is the unusual case introduced earlier: part vitamin, part hormone, and largely made in skin exposed to ultraviolet light rather than eaten. Its central job is to drive calcium absorption from the gut, which makes it foundational to bone, but receptors for it appear in tissues throughout the body. The problem in modern populations is structural. People work indoors, live at latitudes where winter sun is too weak to trigger synthesis for months at a time, use sunscreen that blocks the relevant wavelengths, and, with darker skin, need longer exposure to make the same amount. The dietary backstop is thin: oily fish, egg yolk, and fortified milk are most of the short list, which is exactly why deficiency persists in people eating plenty of calories. Older adults are a particular concern, because aging skin synthesizes vitamin D less efficiently just as the need to protect bone is rising.

Low vitamin D rarely announces itself dramatically in an adult. It tends to show up as the slow background problems: bone that demineralizes over years, muscle weakness, and, because the deficiency is so widespread and tied to bone and falls, a meaningful contributor to fracture risk in the elderly. This is one of the few micronutrients where supplementation is genuinely common and often appropriate, but, as with all the fat-soluble vitamins, the dose is something to confirm rather than guess, because the body stores what it does not use.

Vitamin B12: The Slow-Failing Vitamin

Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is the cofactor for two reactions that matter enormously: one that keeps the nervous system's myelin insulation intact, and one tied into the folate pathway that builds DNA, including the DNA needed to manufacture red blood cells. Run low and two systems suffer in parallel: blood (a specific anemia, with abnormally large red cells) and nerves (numbness, tingling, balance problems, and in severe, prolonged cases, irreversible spinal cord damage). Shipton and Thachil (2015) describe B12 deficiency as common and easy to miss, because its early signs are vague and because the classic cause, an autoimmune condition called pernicious anemia, now accounts for only a minority of cases; far more often the problem is age-related malabsorption of the B12 bound up in food.

Two features make B12 distinctive. First, it is found almost exclusively in animal foods: meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, with fortified foods and supplements as the reliable plant-free routes. That puts strict vegans and long-term vegetarians squarely in the at-risk group, and it is why fortified foods or a supplement are a sensible part of a fully plant-based diet rather than an optional extra. Second, the liver banks a large reserve, often enough for years. That sounds reassuring, and for short stretches it is, but it has a sting: a developing deficiency can stay silent for a long time and then surface as nerve damage that does not fully reverse. The slow timeline is exactly why the problem gets missed. Older adults, people on long-term acid-suppressing medication, those who have had gastric or intestinal surgery, and people with absorption disorders round out the higher-risk groups, because all of them have trouble liberating or absorbing the vitamin even when it is present in the diet (Shipton & Thachil, 2015).

Calcium: Structural Mineral and Cellular Messenger

Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the body, and the overwhelming majority of it is locked into the skeleton, which doubles as a structural frame and a calcium bank. The small fraction circulating in blood and operating inside cells does work out of all proportion to its quantity: it is essential for muscle contraction, nerve signaling, and blood clotting. The body guards blood calcium tightly, and here is the consequence that makes dietary calcium matter over a lifetime. If intake falls short, the body does not let blood calcium drop; instead it withdraws calcium from the bone bank to keep the blood supplied. You feel nothing. The cost is paid in the skeleton, slowly, as bone density erodes.

This is why calcium is best understood as a long-game nutrient. Bone mass is built through childhood and early adulthood and peaks once; what you reach then, plus how well you defend it afterward, sets the trajectory toward, or away from, the thin, fracture-prone bone of osteoporosis later. Calcium does not act alone in this: vitamin D is required to absorb it, and protein and other minerals contribute to the bone matrix, which is one more illustration of nutrients working as a connected system rather than in isolation. Dairy is the densest common source, with canned fish eaten with the bones, certain leafy greens, tofu set with calcium, and fortified foods filling in. Those most at risk of falling short are people who avoid dairy without deliberately replacing it, older adults, and women after menopause, when the loss of estrogen accelerates bone loss.

Magnesium: The Quiet Workhorse Most Diets Underfill

Magnesium is a cofactor for hundreds of enzymatic reactions, and you already met its most fundamental role: virtually every reaction that spends ATP actually spends a magnesium-ATP complex, so magnesium sits at the heart of energy metabolism itself. It is also required for normal muscle and nerve function, blood pressure regulation, and bone structure. Severe, overt magnesium deficiency is uncommon in healthy people, but the more common situation is a chronic, low-grade shortfall. Nielsen (2014) reviews evidence that intakes below roughly 250 milligrams a day, which a meaningful share of the population in industrialized countries falls under, are associated with elevated markers of low-grade inflammation, the kind of subclinical state that contributes over time to chronic disease rather than producing an acute illness you would notice today.

The reason magnesium intake drifts low is dietary structure. The mineral is concentrated in whole, minimally processed plant foods, nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains, and leafy greens, and much of it is stripped out when grains are refined. A diet leaning heavily on refined carbohydrates can hit its calorie targets easily while quietly underfilling magnesium, a clean example of the calorie-rich, micronutrient-poor pattern this chapter keeps circling back to (Nielsen, 2014). The fix is not a pill so much as a shift in the makeup of the plate toward the whole-food sources that carry magnesium in the first place.

Experiments of Nature: The Flagship Deficiencies

We rarely get to run controlled subtraction experiments on human nutrition. History and geography ran them for us. Each flagship deficiency disease is a natural experiment in which one nutrient went missing and revealed its job by the manner of the body's failure. These stories matter not as clinical checklists, diagnosis and treatment belong to clinicians, but because they make abstract roles unforgettable.

Scurvy and Vitamin C: The Collagen Collapse

Return to Anson's sailors. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is the cofactor for the enzymes that hydroxylate proline and lysine residues in collagen, the structural protein that holds together skin, blood vessels, gums, and the matrix of bone. Without functional vitamin C, the body keeps making collagen but cannot stabilize it. The result is exactly what scurvy looks like: gums bleed and recede, capillaries leak, wounds refuse to heal, and, eerily, old, long-healed scars reopen, because the collagen holding them was never replaced (Dresen et al., 2023).

In 1747 the naval surgeon James Lind ran what is often called one of the first controlled clinical trials. He took twelve scorbutic sailors, divided them into pairs, and gave each pair a different putative remedy, cider, vinegar, seawater, citrus, and others. The two men given oranges and lemons recovered so fast that one returned to duty within days. Citrus worked; the rest did not. It still took the Royal Navy decades to adopt the practice broadly, but the link was made (Dresen et al., 2023). Vitamin C is abundant in citrus, peppers, broccoli, strawberries, and many fresh vegetables, which is why the disease vanishes wherever fresh produce is routine.

Rickets and Vitamin D: The Bone That Won't Harden

Vitamin D is unusual: it behaves less like a classic vitamin and more like a hormone, and we can synthesize it in skin exposed to ultraviolet light. Its central metabolic job is to drive intestinal absorption of calcium. Without enough of it, the gut cannot pull in the calcium that bone mineralization requires, and growing bone stays soft. In children this produces rickets, the bowed legs and deformed skeleton historically common in the smoke-darkened, sun-starved cities of the industrial revolution. In adults the equivalent is osteomalacia (Holick, 2007).

Holick's (2007) landmark review makes a sobering point: although food fortification in the twentieth century appeared to "conquer" rickets, vitamin D deficiency remains widespread, driven by indoor lifestyles, sunscreen, latitude, and skin pigmentation that all reduce cutaneous synthesis. The dietary sources are comparatively few, fatty fish, egg yolk, and fortified milk, which is part of why this deficiency persists in modern populations who eat plenty of calories.

Iron-Deficiency Anemia: When Oxygen Can't Get Delivered

If micronutrient deficiency had a global champion, it would be iron. Camaschella (2015) opens her authoritative review by noting that iron-deficiency anemia is the most common form of anemia in the world, affecting a substantial fraction of humanity. Iron sits at the center of hemoglobin, the molecule in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it in the tissues. Run low on iron and you cannot build enough functional hemoglobin; the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity falls.

The symptoms follow directly from the mechanism: fatigue, pallor, breathlessness on exertion, cold intolerance, the signature of tissues quietly starved of oxygen even as the lungs work normally. Iron homeostasis is tightly governed by the hormone hepcidin, which throttles how much iron is absorbed and released (Camaschella, 2015). Dietary iron comes in two forms with very different absorption: heme iron from meat, poultry, and fish is absorbed efficiently, while non-heme iron from beans, lentils, spinach, and fortified grains is absorbed less well, though vitamin C eaten in the same meal markedly boosts its uptake, one of those interconnections Scott-Boyer and colleagues (2016) described.

Think About It

A person eating a plant-based diet pairs their lentils with a squeeze of lemon and some bell pepper. Using two ideas from this chapter, explain why that pairing is more than a flavor choice. Which nutrient is being absorbed, and which cofactor is helping?

Iodine and the Thyroid: A Trace Element That Builds a Hormone

Some nutrients are needed not because they catalyze reactions but because they are literally built into a critical molecule. Iodine is the clearest case. The thyroid hormones T3 and T4, which set the body's metabolic rate, are constructed around iodine atoms (the numbers in their names count those atoms). When dietary iodine falls too low, the thyroid cannot make enough hormone. It compensates by enlarging, producing the visible neck swelling called goitre, and metabolic rate drops (hypothyroidism).

Zimmermann and Boelaert (2015) document the full spectrum of iodine deficiency disorders and emphasize its gravest form: because thyroid hormone is essential for brain development, severe deficiency in pregnancy and early life impairs cognition and growth irreversibly, historically producing the condition once called cretinism. Iodine deficiency was for centuries endemic in mountainous, inland regions whose soils, and therefore crops, were iodine-poor. The modern remedy is one of public health's quiet triumphs: iodized salt, supplemented by seafood and dairy.

Calories Without Nourishment: The Triage Problem

We can now answer the question the chapter opened with: how can a person eating abundant calories still be nutritionally incomplete? The flagship diseases are the dramatic, visible end of that spectrum. But the more common modern situation is subtler, a chronic, low-grade shortfall that never crosses into a named disease yet still degrades how well the body runs.

Bruce Ames (2006) gave this its most influential framing with his triage theory. When micronutrients are in short supply, he argued, the body does not fail randomly. It triages: it allocates the scarce vitamins and minerals to the functions that keep you alive today, immediate energy production, the immune response, and shortchanges the functions whose payoff is decades away, such as DNA repair and the long-term maintenance that protects against cancer and the degenerative diseases of aging. The consequence is insidious. You feel basically fine; no deficiency disease appears; but invisible long-term damage quietly accumulates.

This matters because so many widely eaten foods are energy-rich and micronutrient-poor, heavy in refined carbohydrate and fat, light on the vitamins and minerals that fresh, varied, whole foods carry. A diet can therefore meet or exceed every caloric requirement while leaving the cofactor supply lines chronically thin. Tardy and colleagues (2020) connect precisely these subclinical shortfalls to the everyday complaints, fatigue, low mood, foggy thinking, that rarely get traced back to nutrition at all.

A brief note on context, since this platform trains coaches and educates the general public: the goal here is understanding, not self-diagnosis. Deficiencies are confirmed with clinical testing, and correcting them, especially with supplements, which carry real toxicity risk for the fat-soluble vitamins, is a job for qualified professionals. What this chapter equips you to do is reason about why these nutrients matter and which everyday foods deliver them.

When micronutrients are scarce, nature short-changes the future to pay for the present, a strategy that keeps you alive today at a quiet cost paid decades later.

Paraphrasing the triage theory of Ames (2006)

Enough, Not Megadoses: Why More Is Not Better

The deficiency diseases make a powerful case that too little of a micronutrient is dangerous. It is tempting to run that logic in reverse and conclude that more must be better, that if a little vitamin prevents disease, a lot must build robust health. For micronutrients, that reasoning is wrong, and for some of them it is actively harmful.

The shape of the relationship is the key. Micronutrient benefit follows a curve, not a straight line. Below a threshold you get deficiency. Across a broad middle range you get sufficiency, where adding more buys you nothing because the enzymes that use the nutrient are already saturated; a cell has only so many copies of an enzyme, and once every one has its cofactor, the extra molecules are just along for the ride. Push high enough and you re-enter a danger zone, this time from excess. Nutrition policy captures the top of that curve with the tolerable upper intake level, the highest routine daily intake judged unlikely to cause harm in healthy people. The width between "enough" and "too much" is the safety margin, and it varies enormously by nutrient.

This is where the fat-soluble versus water-soluble split pays off again. Water-soluble vitamins generally have wide margins, because the body excretes the surplus, so toxicity is rare and usually requires sustained, very high supplemental doses. The fat-soluble vitamins, vitamin A in particular, have much narrower margins, because the body stores rather than excretes the excess. Minerals can be tighter still. Strohle and Hahn (2014) make the point sharply with selenium, an essential trace mineral whose gap between the intake needed to prevent deficiency and the intake that begins to cause toxicity is strikingly small; with selenium it really is the dose that makes both the benefit and the poison. Iron is another mineral where excess is a genuine hazard rather than a harmless surplus.

The practical risk is almost never food. It is very hard to reach a toxic intake of any micronutrient by eating ordinary meals, because food delivers nutrients in modest, naturally limited amounts. The risk comes from supplements, and especially from stacking them. Vellos and colleagues (2019) showed how combining a general multivitamin with a separate condition-specific supplement can push intakes of nutrients such as vitamin A and zinc past the tolerable upper limit, not through any single product, but because the doses add up and nobody is keeping a running total. That is the failure mode to understand: not one outrageous megadose, but several reasonable-looking products quietly summing into an unreasonable one.

Bioavailability and the Food Matrix

So far we have mostly talked about how much of a nutrient is present. The harder, more useful question is how much of it your body actually absorbs and uses. That fraction is called bioavailability, and it can differ wildly from the number on a food label or a supplement panel, which reports what went in, not what gets taken up.

You have already seen bioavailability in action. Heme iron from meat is absorbed several times more efficiently than non-heme iron from plants. Vitamin C in the same meal raises non-heme iron uptake, while tea tannins and grain phytates lower it. Fat-soluble vitamins are absorbed poorly from a fat-free meal and well alongside some dietary fat, so the oil on a salad is helping you absorb the vitamins in the vegetables, not just dressing them. These are not curiosities. They are the rule: a nutrient's fate depends heavily on the company it keeps.

This is where the idea of the food matrix earns its place. Whole foods deliver nutrients embedded in a structure, packaged with fiber, fat, protein, and a crowd of other compounds, and that surrounding structure shapes how, how fast, and how completely the nutrients are released and absorbed. The combination often does more than the same nutrients would do in isolation. Geiker and colleagues (2019) reviewed exactly this for dairy and bone, concluding that the effect of dairy on the skeleton is greater than the sum of its individual nutrients, a genuine matrix effect, and arguing that dietary advice should be built around whole foods rather than isolated components for precisely that reason.

This gives a concrete, mechanistic answer to a question people ask constantly: why not skip the produce and take a pill? Sometimes a targeted supplement is the right tool, as with vitamin B12 on a vegan diet or vitamin D in a sun-starved winter. But as a general strategy, isolated nutrients in a capsule lose the matrix. They arrive without the cofactors that travel alongside them in food, without the fiber and fat that pace their absorption, and without the dozens of other compounds whose contributions are real even when they are not fully mapped. The interconnected web of cofactors described earlier is the molecular version of the same point: nutrients are meant to arrive together, in context, and they tend to work best that way.

Get Them From a Varied Diet First

Everything in this chapter converges on a single practical stance: build micronutrient intake from a varied diet of whole and minimally processed foods first, and treat supplements as targeted tools for specific gaps, not as the foundation.

The reasoning is not philosophical, it is mechanistic, and you now have all the pieces. Micronutrients work as an interconnected web, so a varied diet covers gaps you would never think to target individually. Bioavailability and the food matrix mean whole foods often deliver more usable nutrition than the same nutrients isolated in a pill. The safety margin argument means food is nearly impossible to overdose on, while stacked supplements can quietly cross upper limits. And the triage problem means the slow, invisible cost of chronic shortfall is best prevented by the steady, broad coverage that variety provides, not patched after the fact.

Variety is the operative word, because it is variety, not any single celebrated food, that closes the gaps. Different foods carry different nutrients: leafy greens bring folate, magnesium, and vitamin K; colorful fruits and vegetables bring vitamin C and carotenoids; meat, fish, and eggs bring heme iron, B12, and zinc; dairy and fortified foods bring calcium and vitamin D; nuts, seeds, legumes, and whole grains bring magnesium, and more iron and zinc. A plate assembled from a narrow set of foods can look adequate by calories and still leave specific gaps, while a varied one quietly covers nutrients you are not even tracking.

This is not an argument against supplements. There are clear, evidence-based cases for them: vitamin B12 for people eating no animal foods, vitamin D where sun exposure is genuinely inadequate, iron or folate when a clinician identifies a need, and others. The point is the order of operations. Food first, because it brings the whole matrix and is self-limiting; supplements second, aimed at a known gap, ideally confirmed by testing, and kept within sensible limits. Diagnosing a deficiency and prescribing its correction is a clinician's job, especially given the real toxicity risk of the fat-soluble vitamins and some minerals. What this chapter equips you to do is reason clearly about why these nutrients matter and which everyday foods deliver them, so the choices you steer are sound ones.

In Practice

A diet can hit every calorie and macro target and still come up short, because vitamins and minerals work quietly over weeks and months, below the level of daily awareness. This is where variety earns its keep: a plate built from a narrow set of foods can look perfect on paper and still leave gaps.

And know where the lane ends. Spotting that a diet pattern is structurally likely to fall short is education and coaching. Diagnosing a deficiency is a clinician's job. Steer food choices, and refer out for the rest.

Case study: the tired runner who ate well and still ran low

Case

Renee is 29, a software developer who took up distance running two years ago and now trains for half marathons. Eighteen months ago she shifted to a mostly plant-based diet she is genuinely proud of: lentils, tofu, beans, oats, leafy greens, lots of vegetables, plenty of variety, and on paper plenty of calories. For the last few months something has been off. Her easy runs feel hard, her race times have slipped, she is breathless on hills that used to be routine, she feels cold when her colleagues are comfortable, and she is tired in a way that sleep does not fix. She has not changed her training or her sleep. Eating more has not helped. By every habit she associates with health, she is doing things right, and she feels worse.

Run Renee through this chapter and the picture comes into focus. Her calories and even her macronutrients are likely fine; this is not a fuel problem in the ordinary sense. The cluster of symptoms, fatigue that rest does not fix, breathlessness on exertion, cold intolerance, falling endurance, points hard at oxygen delivery, and that points at iron. Her profile stacks several risk factors on top of each other: she menstruates, so she loses iron monthly; she is an endurance athlete, so she loses additional small amounts through sweat, gut, and the breakdown of red cells under repeated impact; and she eats little or no meat, so she relies entirely on non-heme iron.

The diet she is proud of is, ironically, the mechanism. Her iron is coming almost entirely in the non-heme form, the version absorbed least efficiently and most sensitive to the rest of the meal. If she is doing what many health-minded people do, drinking tea or coffee with meals, those tannins are actively cutting her absorption, while the phytates in some of her whole grains and legumes work in the same direction. So she is not simply eating less iron than a meat-eater; she is eating iron in the form whose uptake is easiest to sabotage, and she may be sabotaging it without knowing. The result is a slow erosion of iron stores, then of functional hemoglobin, until her blood's oxygen-carrying capacity drops far enough to show up in exactly the runs that demand the most oxygen.

There is a second nutrient worth flagging, because it travels with this kind of diet. Vitamin B12 is found almost only in animal foods, and if Renee has not added fortified foods or a supplement, her years-long reserve has been quietly drawing down the whole time. B12 deficiency can mimic and compound the fatigue, and because the liver's store is large, it can stay silent until it surfaces as something harder to reverse. This is the interconnected-web idea made personal: one dietary pattern nudges several nutrients in the same direction at once.

The fix is not to abandon the diet, and it is not to eat more in general, which is where Renee was headed. It is targeted and mechanism-driven. On the food side, she can pair her iron-rich plant foods with vitamin C, peppers, citrus, tomatoes, in the same meal to boost non-heme absorption, and move the tea and coffee to between meals rather than alongside them. On the gap side, a plant-based athlete is a textbook case for confirming iron status with a clinician rather than guessing, and for a reliable B12 source through fortified foods or a supplement. That last step matters precisely because iron is a mineral where excess is harmful, so the dose should be guided by testing, not self-prescribed. Same diet she values, a few mechanism-aware adjustments, and the oxygen gets where it needs to go.

Key Takeaways

  • Micronutrients are needed in milligram-to-microgram amounts and supply almost no energy, yet the calorie-scale reactions of metabolism cannot run without them.
  • Minerals are elements (iron, iodine, calcium, magnesium, zinc); vitamins are organic molecules the body cannot make in sufficient quantity. Vitamins split into four fat-soluble (A, D, E, K), stored, with real toxicity risk, and nine water-soluble (B vitamins, C), poorly stored, needing continual resupply, rarely toxic.
  • Most vitamins and minerals act as cofactors: niacin → NAD⁺, riboflavin → FAD, thiamine for pyruvate dehydrogenase, magnesium for ATP, iron for the cytochromes. Remove the milligram-scale cofactor and the pathway stalls.
  • Cofactors work as an interconnected web, not as lone actors, the molecular case for dietary variety (Scott-Boyer et al., 2016).
  • Flagship deficiencies anchor each nutrient to a visible failure: scurvy (vitamin C, collagen), rickets (vitamin D, calcium absorption and bone), iron-deficiency anemia (oxygen delivery), goitre and impaired development (iodine, thyroid hormone).
  • A calorie-adequate diet can still be nutritionally incomplete; the triage theory (Ames, 2006) explains how chronic low intake damages long-term health long before any named deficiency appears.
Looking Ahead

If micronutrients ride along with our food in such variable amounts, the natural next question is which foods carry the most nourishment per calorie. Chapter 5 takes up nutrient density, the central tension between how much energy a food delivers and how much micronutrition comes packaged with it, and gives you a framework for comparing foods not just by their calories, but by everything else they bring along.
